status_notes: Initial research from Google Cloud CDN and Load Balancing documentation, 2026-04-04. Pass 1 (service docs). Needs Pass 2 (API), Pass 3 (cross-reference), and Pass 5 (human review with live GCP environment).
official_name: Google Cloud CDN and Load Balancing
notes: CDN with integrated load balancing. Security includes SSL policies, Cloud Armor integration, signed URLs/cookies, IAP.

match_nodes.description: TLS version and cipher suite control

data.description: SSL policies control the minimum TLS version and cipher suites that the load balancer accepts for HTTPS connections. Applied to target HTTPS proxies. Three predefined profiles plus custom configuration.

options.description: Broadest compatibility. TLS 1.0+, wide cipher suite selection. For clients with outdated TLS libraries.
TLS 1.2+, modern cipher suites. Recommended for most use cases.
TLS 1.2 only, limited cipher suites. For compliance requirements demanding strongest encryption.
Choose specific minimum TLS version and individual cipher suites.

default.note: Default SSL policy if none is explicitly attached to the target HTTPS proxy.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use MODERN profile for most workloads (TLS 1.2+, modern ciphers). Use RESTRICTED for compliance-sensitive workloads. Avoid COMPATIBLE unless legacy client support is required. Monitor SSL policy usage via Cloud Logging.

audit.console: Google Cloud Console → Network services → Load balancing → {LB} → Frontend → SSL policy

match_nodes.description: WAF and DDoS protection at edge

data.description: Google Cloud Armor provides WAF and DDoS protection for applications behind external Application Load Balancers. Supports preconfigured WAF rules (OWASP CRS), custom CEL-based rules, rate limiting, bot management, and adaptive protection (ML-based DDoS detection).
data.vendor_recommendation: Enable Cloud Armor on all public-facing load balancers. Use preconfigured WAF rules as a baseline. Enable adaptive protection (Premium tier) for ML-based DDoS detection. Start with preview mode to evaluate rule impact before enforcement.

audit.console: Google Cloud Console → Network security → Cloud Armor policies

data.description: Restrict access to Cloud CDN cached content using signed URLs or signed cookies. Signed URLs provide time-limited access with a signature based on a shared key. Signed cookies allow access to multiple URLs matching a prefix.

options.description: Time-limited access to a specific URL. Includes expiration, key name, and signature in query parameters.
Time-limited access to URLs matching a URL prefix. Set via Cloud-CDN-Cookie response header.
HMAC keys stored in backend service or bucket configuration. Up to 3 keys for rotation.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use signed URLs for individual file access (downloads, assets). Use signed cookies for multi-resource access (video streaming). Rotate signing keys regularly using the key rotation mechanism (maintain 2 active keys during rotation).

audit.note: Non-empty signedUrlKeyNames indicates signed URLs/cookies are configured.
audit.console: Google Cloud Console → Network services → Load balancing → {LB} → Backend → CDN policy

match_nodes.description: Identity-Aware Proxy for authenticated access to backends

data.description: Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) provides application-level access control for backends behind load balancers. IAP verifies user identity and context before allowing access. Supports Google accounts, workforce identity federation, and BeyondCorp context-aware access.

options.description: Google sign-in, workforce identity, or external identity providers
Access levels based on device attributes, IP, user identity (BeyondCorp)
Service accounts can access IAP-protected resources with OIDC tokens

data.vendor_recommendation: Use IAP for internal applications that should not be publicly accessible without authentication. IAP works at the application layer (L7) — ensure backends also validate the IAP JWT header to prevent bypass via direct access.

audit.note: Check iap.enabled field. True means IAP is enabled for the backend.
audit.console: Google Cloud Console → Security → Identity-Aware Proxy

data.description: Google-managed SSL certificates are auto-provisioned and auto-renewed by Google. Support domain validation (DV) certificates. Can be used with external Application Load Balancers. Certificate Manager provides advanced features for certificate lifecycle management.

options.description: Auto-provisioned DV certificate. Domain validation via DNS or HTTP challenge.
Advanced certificate management. DNS authorization, certificate maps, wildcard support.
Upload your own certificate and private key.

default.note: Certificates must be explicitly created and attached to the target HTTPS proxy.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use Google-managed certificates for simplicity. Use Certificate Manager for advanced features (DNS authorization for faster provisioning, certificate maps, wildcard certificates). Self-managed certificates only when a specific CA is required.

audit.console: Google Cloud Console → Security → Certificate Manager

data.description: Security settings for backend services behind the load balancer. Includes Cloud Armor security policy attachment, request logging, connection draining, health checks, and outlier detection. Controls how the load balancer interacts with backend instances.

options.description: Attach a Cloud Armor policy for WAF and DDoS protection
Enable request logging for the backend service. Configurable sample rate.
Graceful handling of in-flight requests when backends are removed. Configurable timeout.
Monitor backend instance health. Options: HTTP, HTTPS, TCP, SSL, HTTP/2, gRPC.
Automatically eject unhealthy backends based on error rates.

data.vendor_recommendation: Always attach a Cloud Armor security policy to public-facing backend services. Enable logging for security monitoring and incident response. Configure health checks appropriate to the backend protocol.

audit.console: Google Cloud Console → Network services → Load balancing → {LB} → Backend services
